did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used a deep learning model based on a contrastive language-image pre-training (CLIP) architecture with a ViT-B/16 backbone to classify and differentiate actinic keratosis (AK) from seborrheic keratosis (SK) lesions. The model was trained on a dataset of 2,307 patients and validated on three separate cohorts to assist dermatologists in diagnosis.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper is explicitly labeled as 'TYPE Original Research' and describes a multi-center prospective study involving the training of a deep learning model on a dataset of 2,307 patients and validation across three separate cohorts. It presents original experimental results, including diagnostic performance metrics (AUC, NRI, IDI) and statistical analysis, rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Enhancing dermatological diagnosis for differentiating actinic from seborrheic keratosis using deep learning model
brief_summary: This multi-center prospective study aimed to enhance the differentiation of actinic keratosis (AK) from seborrheic keratosis (SK) using a deep learning model. A contrastive language-image pre-training (CLIP) model with a ViT-B/16 architecture was trained on a dataset of 2,307 patients and validated across three separate cohorts from Center A, Centers B and C, and DermNet. Two dermatologists classified lesions independently before and after viewing the model's predictions to evaluate diagnostic improvement. The DL model achieved high diagnostic performance with AUCs ranging from 0.84 to 0.89 across validation cohorts. Dermatologists' diagnostic accuracy significantly improved with model assistance, particularly for less experienced clinicians, as evidenced by increased AUCs and positive net reclassification indices. The study concludes that integrating this DL model can reduce diagnostic subjectivity and aid in the early detection of precancerous lesions.

lesion_types_studied.details: Precancerous lesion with malignant potential; included in training cohort (N=959) and validation cohorts (N=114, N=58, N=165).
Benign skin growth of keratinocytes; included in training cohort (N=1,348) and validation cohorts (N=272, N=137, N=50).

all_task_types.target: Differentiating Actinic keratosis (AK) from Seborrheic keratosis (SK)
all_task_types.details: Binary classification task to distinguish between two discrete diagnostic categories: Actinic keratosis (precancerous) and Seborrheic keratosis (benign). The model predicts discrete lesion classes using a CLIP architecture with ViT-B/16 backbone, evaluated via AUC, sensitivity, specificity, PPV, and NPV.

datasets_used.name: ISIC Archive
datasets_used.description: A public dataset from the International Skin Imaging Collaboration (ISIC) containing histopathologically confirmed images of skin lesions.

use.training: Used as the training cohort to train the deep learning model.

datasets_used.name: Validation Dataset 1 (Center A)
datasets_used.description: A private cohort of patients from Center A (Shanghai Sixth People's Hospital, Jiaotong University) who underwent surgical resection for skin neoplasms.

use.validation: Used as validation dataset 1 to validate the DL model.

datasets_used.name: Validation Dataset 2 (Centers B and C)
datasets_used.description: A private cohort combining patients from Center B (Jinshan Hospital, Fudan University) and Center C (Shanghai United Family Pudong Hospital) who underwent surgical resection for skin neoplasms.

use.validation: Used as validation dataset 2 to validate the DL model.

datasets_used.name: Validation Dataset 3 (DermNet)
datasets_used.description: A public dataset from DermNet (https://dermnetnz.org/images) containing histopathologically confirmed clinical photographs.

use.validation: Used as validation dataset 3 to validate the DL model.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To compare continuous variables that met the assumptions of normality.
To compare continuous variables that did not meet the assumptions of normality.
To analyze the change in dermatologists’ classification decisions and evaluate diagnostic improvement with model assistance.
To evaluate the improvement in discrimination between AK and SK with model assistance.
To report uncertainty for Area Under the Curve (AUC) metrics.
To determine statistical significance for all reported tests.

hypothesis_testing.test: Independent-samples t-test, Mann–Whitney U test, Chi-squared test, Fisher’s exact test, and Net Reclassification Index (NRI) / Integrated Discrimination Index (IDI) with associated p-values.
hypothesis_testing.null_hypothesis: There is no difference in the distribution of continuous variables between groups (for t-test/Mann-Whitney), no association between categorical variables (for Chi-squared/Fisher's), and no improvement in diagnostic classification accuracy when dermatologists use the deep learning model compared to their baseline (for NRI/IDI).
hypothesis_testing.alternative_hypothesis: There is a significant difference in the distribution of continuous variables between groups, an association between categorical variables, or a significant improvement in diagnostic classification accuracy when dermatologists use the deep learning model compared to their baseline.
hypothesis_testing.interpretation: A p-value less than 0.05 was considered statistically significant. The study reported significant improvements in dermatologists' diagnostic performance (AUC, NRI, IDI) with model assistance, with specific p-values reported for NRI and IDI improvements (e.g., p=0.006, p<0.001).

Training Cohort (ISIC Dataset).notes: The model demonstrated an AUC of 0.85. The training cohort included 2,307 patients (1,348 SK, 959 AK).

Validation Cohort 1 (Center A).notes: Validation cohort 1 included 386 patients (272 SK, 114 AK). Dermatologist 1 had 15 years experience; Dermatologist 2 had 3 years experience. NRI for Dermatologist 1 was 0.10 (p=0.006). NRI for Dermatologist 2 was 0.19 (p<0.001). IDI for Dermatologist 1 was 0.14 (p<0.001). IDI for Dermatologist 2 was 0.27 (p<0.001).

Validation Cohort 2 (Centers B and C).notes: Validation cohort 2 included 196 patients (137 SK, 58 AK). Note: Text mentions 195 patients in one section but 196 in abstract; Table 1 lists N=137+58=195. The abstract states 196. Table 2 lists specific counts. The text states 'validation dataset2 included a total of 195 patients'. Table 1 lists SK=137, AK=58. Total 195. The abstract says 196. We use the detailed table data where available. The text says 'validation cohort2 (Center B and C)... AUC of 0.84'. Table 2 confirms AUC 0.84 for CLIP. NRI/IDI values were calculated on a merged dataset of Center A-C.

Validation Cohort 3 (DermNet).notes: Validation cohort 3 included 215 patients (50 SK, 165 AK). The model achieved an AUC of 0.89. Dermatologist performance metrics for this specific cohort were not explicitly listed in Table 2, but the abstract mentions overall improvement for dermatologists.

architecture_used.main_model: Contrastive Language-Image Pre-training (CLIP) model with Vision Transformer (ViT-B/16) backbone

architecture_details.backbone: ViT-B/16 (Vision Transformer Base with 16x16 patch size)
architecture_details.mechanism: Images divided into fixed-size patches, flattened, embedded into vectors, position encodings added, passed through Transformer encoder with self-attention and fully connected feed-forward networks, feature pooling, and projection to text embedding space
architecture_details.prompt_learning: Number of learnable prompt vectors set to 16

training_details.hardware: Two NVIDIA RTX 4090 GPUs (24GB memory each), Intel Core i9-13900K CPU, 256 GB DDR4 RAM

classification_or_output_head.output_processing: Softmax function to transform outputs into probability distribution

preprocessing_techniques: Image resizing to a standard size suitable for the DL model input layer
Conversion of images to tensor format
Data augmentation including random cropping, rotation, flipping, and color transformations
Normalization by subtracting the mean value of the entire dataset and dividing by the standard deviation to normalize pixel values

conclusions: The CLIP-based ViT deep learning model substantially enhanced dermatologists' ability to distinguish actinic keratosis (AK) from seborrheic keratosis (SK).
Less experienced dermatologists benefited more significantly from the model's assistance compared to more experienced ones.
The DL model has the potential to reduce diagnostic subjectivity in clinical settings.
The model aids in the early identification of precancerous lesions (AK).
Integrating DL models into dermatological practice holds promise for revolutionizing diagnostic approaches and refining therapeutic strategies.
Future research is needed to conduct prospective, longitudinal evaluations in clinical workflows across institutions to assess practical value and sustainability.
Expanding dataset diversity is essential to validate the model's robustness across broader populations and ensure fairness and clinical applicability worldwide.
The model's ability to handle malignant or high-risk lesions for more urgent patient outcome impact and its integration into routine practice remain untested.
important_remarks: The study acknowledges a restricted dataset scope, lacking multi-ethnic and international diversity, which limits the model's generalizability and robustness across broader populations.
The model lacks longitudinal and real-world validation; its long-term stability, impact on patient outcomes, and integration into routine clinical practice remain untested.

Attention map analysis revealed that the model occasionally focused on non-lesion areas (background skin, lighting variations, or framing artifacts), raising concerns about reliance on spurious features rather than clinically relevant lesion characteristics.
The study did not report performance metrics for dermatologists on Validation Cohort 3 (DermNet), limiting the assessment of model assistance on this specific external public dataset.
There is a discrepancy in the reported size of Validation Cohort 2, with the abstract stating 196 patients while the detailed text and table data indicate 195 patients.
The model's ability to handle malignant or high-risk lesions for urgent patient outcome impact was not evaluated in this study.
The study was conducted at three centers in Shanghai and one public dataset (DermNet), potentially introducing geographic and demographic bias not representative of global dermatological populations.
